problem_statement: Knowledge base search is using prefix-style matching, so articles are only returned when the query appears at the start of topic/title/content. Queries that match text in the middle of a field are not found.
goal: Update knowledge base search so it performs a contains/substring match across topic, title, and content, returning articles whenever the query appears anywhere in those fields.
acceptance_criteria: Searching for a term that appears mid-sentence in an article's topic/title/content returns that article.
Existing prefix matches continue to work as part of the broader contains match.
Search behavior is consistent across all knowledge article fields used by the search endpoint.
constraints: Smallest likely change is in backend/app/services/knowledge_service.py, specifically the search implementation.
Prefer preserving current API contract and result shape unless the search logic requires otherwise.
unknowns: Whether search is implemented with SQL LIKE/ILIKE, Python string matching, or ORM filters in knowledge_service.py.
Whether any related tests already cover search semantics and need adjustment.
repo_summary: Repository contains backend app with knowledge service, API routes, models, schemas, and tests. The issue points directly to the knowledge service search implementation.

targeted_symbols.reason: This is the affected implementation area named in the issue; likely contains the prefix-only filtering logic that must be changed to substring/contains matching.
Search semantics should be validated here; existing tests likely need updates or a new contains-match test to prevent regressions.

localization_rationale: The issue explicitly names backend/app/services/knowledge_service.py (search) as the affected file.
The symptom is purely search semantics, so the smallest relevant implementation area is the service method that constructs article filters, not routes, schemas, or models.
A corresponding service test file is present and is the most likely place to confirm or adapt the expected matching behavior.
